Because scared/worried/concerned is too vague entirely.

do you think this repetition is worth keeping? I'm not sure how we can handle this in english as the CN version has it in mandarin and english - it feels a little redundant in EN
Written like so (copied from source sheet):
那持灯的预备了火焰，要将一切罪恶从世间驱逐。
the Light Bearer hath prepared a fire, to burn wickedness from this world.

I feel like we could have something like:
The Light Bearer hath prepared a fire to drive all wickedness, to _burn_ all wickedness, from this world.

If you have any other ideas I'm welcome to it!

two options really, either we just remove the chinese part entirely since it's redundant and doesn't really contribute anything
or we preserve the spirit of the original, which is that the chinese text repeated the phrase in a foreign language that's unintelligible to most players but sounds and looks really cool
the closest equivalent we have to that in english (imo) is latin, so you could just chatgpt the phrase into latin and we'll have repeated history
also does help in that there's multiple precedents in the existing english of using latin as the cool foreign language equivalent, plus Latin has a lot of ties to Christian religious stuff anyway
The latin option does sound good, and does fit with the religious imagery forsure
I'll see how it looks
plugged it into chatgpt (because somehow I kind of get the feeling we're not going to find an expert on Latin amidst our ranks):

so I suppose it'd look something like:
> The Light Bearer hath prepared a fire to burn all wickedness from this world.
> Luminifer ignem paravit, quo ex hoc mundo omnis iniquitas comburatur.

fellas: stupid question time. Didn't want to reply directly to Ace here in case this creeps into rude question territory.
> I understand you want to flavor up the text, but we will have to ask you to refrain from doing it now
> (...)because we as devs would be unable to make sure your stylistic upgrade is consistently applied to the entire game.
> 
presupposing that it's unimportant exactly what things I choose to spend my own time doing, would it be wrong to just... do it anyway?
From what I understand of github, the pull requests are evaluated and implemented on an individual basis, no? So if I'm confident enough in my ability to be thorough enough to confirm my "stylistic upgrade is consistently applied to the entire game" (which I absolutely am <:wanda_smug:1408324303912439878>) I can just make that collection of changes its own pull request to be evaluated and *probably not* implemented anyway right?

The only two issues I see are that (1) the devs wouldn't be "in on it" that I'm making this kind of change (in this case Entourage), and continue to put out new work that doesn't comply with those changes, and (b) because of the group nature of the project, it would be potentially difficult to confirm that none of this work ends up overlapping and continuing to be inconsistent.
But I'm very aware of problem (a) and it's reflective on basically my entire personal project, so...

The way I see it, it's all volunteer work so unless someone needs *me specifically* to work on something in particular, I may as well proceed on whatever I want to do whether they choose to use it or not right?
My ultimate aim isn't to get anything into the game by the next update, it's to help advise and collaborate with whoever in biav chooses to listen, present or future.
-# the daydream scenario for me is that nothing I do gets implemented at all until the new hire gets settled in and goes to touch the gameplay templating, and checks over my work to realize I did most of their job for them lol but that's probably just me with my head up my ass again.
So fellas, should I continue with my personal project here, breaking each change category into its own pull, only when it's 110% ready? or am I out of line, and should focus first on the things they're asking to have done first?
this is taking a lot more effort than I thought but I still think I can finish all of act 1 before kath banner ends
this is a fairly long response so it will be a big wall, apologies

> presupposing that it's unimportant exactly what things I choose to spend my own time doing, would it be wrong to just... do it anyway?
not if you do it on your own time with your own resources, but personally I do think there is a sort of implicit rudeness to spend most of your time working on non-project-related tasks if you were recruited to help with a project (of course, if you still contribute signficantly to the project while also working on it on your own time, I certainly don't see any issue with that)

> From what I understand of github, the pull requests are evaluated and implemented on an individual basis, no? So if I'm confident enough in my ability to be thorough enough to confirm my "stylistic upgrade is consistently applied to the entire game" (which I absolutely am <:wanda_smug:1408324303912439878>) I can just make that collection of changes its own pull request to be evaluated and probably not implemented anyway right?
technically yes but I would caution against actually submitting it as a pull request, since Ace and staff seem to be using it primarily as a version tracking system and not in the proper git code management system - i.e., submitting a pull request means Ace would probably have to manually check through all the files and then reference each line to figure out where the changes were made and what impact it'd have, which is a lot of work to put on someone who isn't expecting it. 
(evidence to support this: us being requested to name files accordingly so the devs can match it to the content, rather than being able to simply locate it via any sort of automated tracker - this implies they're doing some level of manual verification)

you are also putting the onus on her to reject the pull request and explain why, in addition to setting a precedent of forcing her to double-check and verify individual pull requests in detail rather than good-will assuming all changes are made in good faith and auto-approving pull requests (it's possible she could already be doing so, but I do feel the spirit of the project is that all contributions are positive in some way, and I would imagine her workload is large enough without having to individually check all of our work in addition to her normal workload)

note that my only concern is actually submitting a pull request - if you work on stuff in a separate branch of your forked project without ever submitting a pull request, I see absolutely no problem with that. 

> The only two issues I see are that (1) the devs wouldn't be "in on it" that I'm making this kind of change (in this case Entourage), and continue to put out new work that doesn't comply with those changes, and (b) because of the group nature of the project, it would be potentially difficult to confirm that none of this work ends up overlapping and continuing to be inconsistent.
> But I'm very aware of problem (a) and it's reflective on basically my entire personal project, so...
true but I do feel like you're glossing over the impact of that. As someone who's worked on live-service projects before, the idea of "well there's not much difference now so I can just do x and then update it to reflect new developments whenever they come out" can quickly spiral into a vast gulf of difference as your work begins to separate more and more from the existing work. I'm not saying you aren't capable or haven't thought things through, I'm just saying that most instances of this I've seen have ended in failure more often than they have success (my own experience included), so it's just a word of caution.

> The way I see it, it's all volunteer work so unless someone needs me specifically to work on something in particular, I may as well proceed on whatever I want to do whether they choose to use it or not right?
I mean yesssss but we're also quite a small group of people working on quite a large project. It's been like almost a week and so far there's been almost zero progress made on gameplay edits whatsoever, outside of some proofreading/editing Goz did on a few cards and the work Dredge did on Horla's cards. I do feel like it may be more beneficial to the group to contribute with doing an immediate first pass cleanup to make the cards and text better, and then working on more expansive projects afterwards.

> My ultimate aim isn't to get anything into the game by the next update, it's to help advise and collaborate with whoever in biav chooses to listen, present or future.
> the daydream scenario for me is that nothing I do gets implemented at all until the new hire gets settled in and goes to touch the gameplay templating, and checks over my work to realize I did most of their job for them lol but that's probably just me with my head up my ass again.
> So fellas, am I out of line, or should I continue with my personal project here, breaking each major change into its own pull, only when it's 110% ready?
One thing I do wish to say (and please don't take it negatively or as a personal attack - I simply lack the social nuance to word this properly) is that you may be somewhat over-estimating your (our) importance to BIAV. Given the sheer amount of work they need to do, the tight timelines they themselves are working under (Light mentioned that the writing for a new event gets finished *hours* before it goes live), and the fact that we have no direct dev pipeline or contact with a designer/developer/staff member outside of Ace as the project lead, leads me to believe that they do not have much (if any) leeway to pay attention to us, nor is too much weight being put on our opinions or feedback. I think we primarily exist to help out under the premise that anything is better than nothing, but most likely are not expected to significantly contribute or have any influence or say in the long-term direction or development of the game.

I think one key component that you may be missing is that implementation is two-way, and regardless of how well of a job you do and how complete the work is, there will need to be significant investment from BIAV's side, both in raw manpower for implementation and developer hours in design/development, to implement *any* level of sweeping reform or system changes. Even if you presented them with a fully perfected, 100% complete total system overhaul on a platter, the fact is that someone at BIAV would need to fully understand it, comprehend it, rework their own understandings to match it, and then abide by that structure going forward, before that project could be incorporated into the game itself. That is a *big ask* for a work that we are not *officially* part of, in terms of development and design. Again, at the risk of sounding quite rude, I personally have not gotten the impression that our opinions matter enough to BIAV for them to be willing to dedicate that amount of time and resources to us. I think Ace's statement is more of a future generalization of "yes this would be quite nice *if* in the future we have the leeway to handle it", but with a strong caveat on the "*if*" part with no guarantee.
done, sorry

Thank you for the response, it's kinda what I was expecting and hoping to hear (and if anyone else has any other opinions please do tell).

> not if you do it on your own time with your own resources, but personally I do think there is a sort of implicit rudeness to spend most of your time working on non-project-related tasks if you were recruited to help with a project (of course, if you still contribute signficantly to the project while also working on it on your own time, I certainly don't see any issue with that)
Sure! that's my plan, and I've basically been jotting down notes as I go along in my own preparation for the big task, but I am actually still doing work on the project at hand *(currently, should be done around <t:1756245600:t>ish with sorting and organizing all of the lines for Wheels of Destiny for y'all to have easier access to)*, and have been doing corrections on the current state of things in line with the project goal in each category and plan to present those long before any sweeping changes.

> note that my only concern is actually submitting a pull request - if you work on stuff in a separate branch of your forked project without ever submitting a pull request, I see absolutely no problem with that. 
Got it! Still learning how github works lol. Thanks!

> It's been like almost a week and so far there's been almost zero progress made on gameplay edits whatsoever,
That actually brings me a little comfort in my choice of priorities, which currently is making the "Gameplay" sheet legible, which if I put about 12% more effort into, will hopefully be helpful for everyone else here to use when they get around to trying to proofread their language's Gameplay sheet.

> One thing I do wish to say (...) is that you may be somewhat over-estimating your (our) importance to BIAV. (...)
> I think we primarily exist to help out under the premise that anything is better than nothing, but most likely are not expected to significantly contribute or have any influence or say in the long-term direction or development of the game. (...)
> I think Ace's statement is more of a future generalization of "yes this would be quite nice *if* in the future we have the leeway to handle it", but with a strong caveat on the *"if"* part with no guarantee.
Okay! I don't necessarily think that's a bad thing at all, honestly. Again, I aim to, with the tools and recognition they've given me, prove my worth even if only to myself, and *"aim for the stars, shoot for the moon"* by *"measuring thrice and cutting once."*
If truly nothing else, the time I'm putting into this will make for a banger wiki.

On further consideration I still think my words might have sounded overly harsh, so I just want to clear up that I'm not trying to rain on your parade or anything, I just fear that much of your work may be spent in vain, and as someone who wants to see this project succeed greatly, I am quite significantly invested in your work as well as mine.

In short, my main concern is that you may be falling in some part for a trap described most aptly by "perfect is the enemy of good" (Voltaire). The concept of a large, all-encompassing perfect framework is incredibly attractive and I certainly understand the appeal of a completed work, but beelining *too* hard on the insistence of perfection often serves as a detriment to the implementation of work that, while not perfect or beautiful, can still contribute a significant positive influence.

One thing you said did resonate with me, which was:
> My ultimate aim...(is) to help advise and collaborate with whoever in biav chooses to listen, present or future.
I also have the same ultimate goal - however, from my point of view, I believe an important pre-requisite to the ability to implement such large-scale work is to first have the influence with BIAV to do so. As I currently don't believe we have that, I would suggest that the most optimal path to obtaining it would be to demonstrate our effectiveness via tangible, verifiable benefits.

For example, my immediate personal goal at this point in time is to transform the entirety of the first story act into accurately translated proper English - if not at a level of high prose, then at least at a level where the reading experience is natural enough that people consider it a properly written work rather than leaving a negative review on Steam about how badly it reads. By achieving that, my belief is that I can demonstrably prove the significant effect that my contributions have had on the game, and as such I hope to receive a corresponding boost in valuation from BIAV, to the extent that I can be trusted to do good work. Continuous iteration on this cycle should both elevate the quality of the game over time, and improve my reputation to the point where my goal in the indeterminate future is to establish some level of communication with a narrative designer or writer, so that I can better understand certain contexts and intents to create a more accurate, high-quality translation.

Similarly, as this is a community group project, the same applies to all of the work we as a team do. I want everyone to contribute meaningfully and positively, as it will raise the value of our voice as a group to BIAV - if we as a group can raise the quality of the game to a point where BIAV see a considerable and tangible improvement in metrics such as player retention and new player attraction, I strongly believe that they will be much more willing to interface with us directly and potentially hand us more responsibility (as we will have demonstrated our value to them).

As such, I think there is immense value in visible, short-term work by the group as a whole so that existing players can see our impact on the game, and BIAV can understand the influence we hold in helping them build a better game. By stacking up contributions over time and gradually improving the polish and quality of the game, we can create a positive feedback loop where both players and devs can see improvements to the game at a visible rate, and will thusly believe that our work will continue to improve the game. This is going to be a group project, and I believe unity and cohesiveness is *incredibly* important in presenting a united front. The higher quality our output as a group, the greater the benefits we will see.

The flip side is that if we do *not* provide visible, tangible results, the natural result is a lowering of the expectations of us and correspondingly the impact we will have. A key example to point towards is Ace's post earlier on that he and Light do not expect us to be consistent, and that much of the work expected from us is more of a basic rewording/editing than it is quality translation work. I think this really exemplifies their current lack of expectations in us, but I also believe that if we can provide them with something amazing, that will all the more serve to subvert their expectations and show them our worth as a group.

With regards to your personal project, I think that immediately tackling work on baseline standardization is important, even if there is not a fully constructed framework to catch everything, or if certain terms are not fully covered. Even something as simple as changing every instance of "Obtain X shield" to "Gain X shield" that you find can be a very visible change - same for things like "Apply"ing debuffs or "Inflict"ing poison/counter. Removing awkward wordings which only show up on a few cards, such as "enjoys X", or the inconsistencies with Tentacle Damage scaling skills, is going to alleviate a lot of the confusion we see in the playerbase and jokes about bad skill descriptions.

These changes do not have to be perfect, and they do not need to be all-encompassing - they just need to put our work on the board, so that people can see gradual improvements and fixes over time. I think, speaking from personal experience, that if we can demonstrably prove our impact on the game, the players themselves will start to come forward with suggestions and instances of things we may have missed or may have done incorrectly. We may also get suggestions or considerations that we did not think of ourselves, which would work better than the terms we chose.

One last thing that I think is quite important - as someone who has personally worked on creating a standardized, consistent framework for in-game skill effects, nothing will contribute more towards the creation of that framework than actively getting in and beginning actual work on the skills themselves. I think that once you begin actively editing the cards, you'll find that there are significantly more fringe cases and exceptions that you did not consider or account for. Rather than trying to build a framework constantly the entire time and having to go back and revisit it over and over multiple times every time you find an exception, you will probably be much better suited to creating that framework once you have worked on hundreds of skill cards and understood the system in significantly more detail - this will provide much more necessary context, and once you begin planning a framework, it will account for all of the various things you have already seen and be much more flexible.

Going on the to-do list...

I mean I know it's annoying because it doesn't fit the usual use case, but even with the current version, you can just do lines with the first change, commit, then add lines with the second change & apply it to the lines of the first batch that need it, then continue liek that.

Meaning if you ever need to change something that's part of the second commit (say "gain" for arith gets replaced), you have a commit whose content is just those edits, you can check the diff to make sure you're not missing anything, you can also just edit it instead of amking another commit on top (just rebase -i on the start/previous one and edit that commit)

What I mean is that at (hopefully) no cost to you apart form ordering your changes, you could have a way to make sure you can go back on/touch up/cancel any number of them easily

PS : *due to the way this repo is organised, you won't be able to reorder the commits though unless you have an initial commit with every line you're ever gonna touch*
